nodes.label: Akira Kurosawa
nodes.summary: Japanese filmmaker (1910–1998) whose samurai epics reshaped world cinema.

nodes.label: The Hidden Fortress
nodes.summary: Akira Kurosawa's 1958 jidaigeki adventure film.

nodes.label: Star Wars (film)
nodes.summary: George Lucas's 1977 space-opera film.

nodes.label: George Lucas
nodes.summary: American filmmaker (b. 1944), creator of Star Wars.

nodes.label: Joseph Campbell
nodes.summary: American mythologist (1904–1987) who formulated the 'hero's journey' monomyth.

nodes.label: The Beatles
nodes.summary: English rock band (1960–1970).

nodes.label: Ravi Shankar
nodes.summary: Indian sitar virtuoso (1920–2012) who brought Hindustani classical music to the West.

nodes.label: Indian classical music
nodes.summary: The art-music tradition of the Indian subcontinent, with roots in Vedic chant.

nodes.label: blues
nodes.summary: African-American vocal and instrumental music that arose in the Deep South around 1900.

nodes.label: jazz
nodes.summary: African-American music that emerged in New Orleans in the early 20th century.

nodes.label: rock and roll
nodes.summary: Popular music that emerged in the United States in the early 1950s.

nodes.label: Alan Turing
nodes.summary: English mathematician (1912–1954), founder of theoretical computer science.

nodes.label: computer
nodes.summary: A programmable machine that carries out sequences of arithmetic or logical operations.

nodes.label: transistor
nodes.summary: The semiconductor switching device (invented 1947) that underpins all modern electronics.

nodes.label: civil rights movement
nodes.summary: The 1954–1968 U.S. non-violent movement for African-American civil rights.

nodes.label: Martin Luther King Jr.
nodes.summary: American civil-rights leader (1929–1968).

nodes.label: Mahatma Gandhi
nodes.summary: Leader of India's non-violent independence movement (1869–1948).

nodes.label: Seven Samurai
nodes.summary: Akira Kurosawa's 1954 epic, among the most influential films ever made.

nodes.label: Rashomon
nodes.summary: Akira Kurosawa's 1950 film, source of the phrase 'the Rashomon effect'.

nodes.label: The Magnificent Seven
nodes.summary: The 1960 American Western remake of Kurosawa's Seven Samurai.

nodes.label: gospel music
nodes.summary: African-American Christian devotional music that shaped soul and the civil-rights freedom songs.

nodes.label: Soul music
nodes.summary: African-American popular music that grew out of gospel and rhythm and blues.

nodes.label: funk
nodes.summary: Rhythm-driven African-American music that emerged from soul and jazz in the mid-1960s.

nodes.label: Hip hop music
nodes.summary: Music built from looped breakbeats and rhyming speech, originating in the 1970s Bronx.

nodes.label: Bob Dylan
nodes.summary: American singer-songwriter (b. 1941) who brought literary lyricism to popular music.

nodes.label: Internet
nodes.summary: The global network of interconnected computers, grown from 1960s packet-switching research.

nodes.label: television
nodes.summary: The mass broadcast medium for moving images, dominant from the mid-20th century.

statements.subject: star_wars
statements.evidence: Star Wars (1977) was written and directed by George Lucas.
statements.headline: Star Wars was written and directed by George Lucas.

statements.subject: star_wars
statements.evidence: George Lucas has repeatedly cited Kurosawa's The Hidden Fortress as an influence on Star Wars, including telling the story through two low-status bickering characters.
statements.headline: George Lucas modelled Star Wars partly on Kurosawa's samurai film The Hidden Fortress.

statements.subject: the_hidden_fortress
statements.evidence: The Hidden Fortress (1958) was directed and co-written by Akira Kurosawa.
statements.headline: The Hidden Fortress is a 1958 samurai adventure directed by Akira Kurosawa.

statements.subject: george_lucas
statements.evidence: George Lucas credited Joseph Campbell's monomyth, the 'hero's journey', as a framework for Star Wars and called Campbell a mentor.
statements.headline: George Lucas built Star Wars on Joseph Campbell's 'hero's journey' monomyth.

statements.subject: star_wars
statements.evidence: Star Wars (1977) pioneered computer-controlled motion-control photography, and Lucas's ILM later drove the rise of digital visual effects.
statements.headline: Star Wars pioneered computer-controlled cameras and helped launch digital visual effects.

statements.subject: computer
statements.evidence: The modern stored-program computer realises Alan Turing's 1936 concept of a 'universal machine'.
statements.headline: Every modern computer is a physical realisation of Alan Turing's 1936 'universal machine'.

statements.subject: transistor
statements.evidence: The transistor replaced the vacuum tube as the fundamental switching element of the modern computer.
statements.headline: The transistor is the building block every modern computer is made of.

statements.subject: jazz
statements.evidence: Jazz grew out of the blues and ragtime in early-20th-century African-American communities.
statements.headline: Jazz grew out of the blues in the African-American South.

statements.subject: rock_and_roll
statements.evidence: Rock and roll developed in the 1950s out of rhythm and blues, itself a descendant of the blues.
statements.headline: Rock and roll grew directly out of African-American rhythm and blues.

statements.subject: the_beatles
statements.evidence: The Beatles began as a rock and roll band, covering 1950s American rock and roll before writing their own songs.
statements.headline: The Beatles started out as a 1950s rock and roll cover band.

statements.subject: the_beatles
statements.evidence: George Harrison studied the sitar under Ravi Shankar, bringing Indian classical music into the Beatles' later work.
statements.headline: The Beatles' Indian-music phase came from George Harrison studying sitar under Ravi Shankar.

statements.subject: ravi_shankar
statements.evidence: Ravi Shankar was a master of Hindustani classical music, the North Indian art-music tradition.
statements.headline: Ravi Shankar was a grandmaster of the North Indian classical sitar.

statements.subject: rock_and_roll
statements.evidence: The cheap transistor radio of the 1950s spread rock and roll to a mass teenage audience, turning it into a youth phenomenon.
statements.headline: The transistor radio carried rock and roll into every 1950s teenager's pocket.

statements.subject: civil_rights_movement
statements.evidence: Jazz and its musicians were woven into the American civil-rights movement as an assertion of Black cultural pride, from Nina Simone's protest songs to benefit concerts.
statements.headline: Jazz became a musical voice of the American civil-rights movement.

statements.subject: martin_luther_king
statements.evidence: Martin Luther King Jr. was the most prominent leader of the American civil-rights movement.
statements.headline: Martin Luther King Jr. led the American civil-rights movement.

statements.subject: martin_luther_king
statements.evidence: Martin Luther King Jr. adopted Gandhi's method of non-violent civil disobedience as the core strategy of the American civil-rights movement.
statements.headline: Martin Luther King Jr. took his non-violent strategy from Mahatma Gandhi.

statements.subject: seven_samurai
statements.evidence: Seven Samurai (1954) was directed and co-written by Akira Kurosawa.
statements.headline: Seven Samurai is Akira Kurosawa's 1954 masterpiece.

statements.subject: rashomon
statements.evidence: Rashomon (1950) was directed by Akira Kurosawa and gave English the phrase 'the Rashomon effect'.
statements.headline: Kurosawa's Rashomon gave English the phrase 'the Rashomon effect'.

statements.subject: the_magnificent_seven
statements.evidence: The Magnificent Seven (1960) is a Hollywood Western re-telling of Kurosawa's Seven Samurai.
statements.headline: The Magnificent Seven is a Hollywood Western remake of Kurosawa's Seven Samurai.

statements.subject: civil_rights_movement
statements.evidence: The freedom songs that carried the civil-rights movement were largely adapted from Black gospel hymns.
statements.headline: The civil-rights movement's freedom songs came out of Black gospel music.

statements.subject: soul_music
statements.evidence: Soul music grew directly out of Black gospel music in the late 1950s, keeping its vocal fervour.
statements.headline: Soul music grew directly out of Black gospel music.

statements.subject: the_beatles
statements.evidence: The Beatles' early repertoire drew heavily on American soul and Motown records.
statements.headline: The Beatles' early sound leaned on American soul and Motown records.

statements.subject: funk
statements.evidence: Funk emerged in the mid-1960s out of soul, jazz and R&B, built on syncopated basslines and grooves.
statements.headline: Funk emerged out of soul, jazz and R&B in the mid-1960s.

statements.subject: hip_hop
statements.evidence: Hip hop was built in the 1970s Bronx from funk and soul breakbeats looped by DJs.
statements.headline: Hip hop was built from funk and soul breakbeats in the 1970s Bronx.

statements.subject: the_beatles
statements.evidence: From 1964 Bob Dylan pushed the Beatles toward introspective, literary lyrics.
statements.headline: Bob Dylan pushed the Beatles toward introspective, literary songwriting.

statements.subject: the_internet
statements.evidence: The Internet is a global network of interconnected computers, grown from 1960s packet-switching research.
statements.headline: The Internet is a global network built by wiring computers together.

statements.subject: television
statements.evidence: The transistor made the television set compact, low-power and cheap enough for every home.
statements.headline: The transistor turned television into a compact mass-market appliance.
